mappings.rationale: Directly covers the adaptive significance of sex (B.3), sex patterns (B.5), sexual dimorphism, mating mechanisms, and the evolution of reproduction (A.3).
Covers sex chromosomes and sex linkage (B.6.a), cell division including meiosis (A.1.b), and the Red Queen hypothesis relevant to the origin of sex (B.4).
Covers cell reproduction via binary fission (A.1.b), the nuclear envelope and nucleus during cell division, and the cellular basis of gamete formation.
Directly covers plant reproductive systems (C) including pollen, pollination, seed plants, and the diversity of reproductive structures in bryophytes and tracheophytes.
Addresses hormonal influences on reproduction (B.6.d, D.3.c) including reproductive cycles, releasing hormones, and the pituitary-gonadal axis.
Covers natural selection and reproduction (A.3), the evolution of sex and genetic diversity (B.3), and genetic drift in reproductive populations.
Covers invertebrate reproductive system diversity (D.2) across phyla including Annelida, Cnidaria, Echinodermata, and Platyhelminthes.
Addresses gamete formation, fertilization, and provisions for developing embryos (D.2.d, D.3.d) including identical twins and parthenogenesis.
Covers both sexual and asexual reproductive strategies in fungi (A.2), including spore formation and parasitism as reproductive adaptations.
Covers transduction and transformation as sexlike recombination in bacteria (B.2) and binary fission as a mode of cell reproduction (A.1.b).
Covers reproduction in microorganisms including algae, archaea, and bacteria, addressing molecular replication (A.1.a) and diverse life cycles (A.1.d).
Addresses the genetic basis of sex determination (B.6) and the relationship between reproduction, heredity, and variation control (A.3).
Covers parental care and mating behaviours as reproductive adaptations (D.2.b, D.3.b), and kin selection in reproductive strategies.
Addresses the distinctions between sex, sexuality, and reproduction (B.1) from biological and social perspectives.
